```{r, echo=FALSE, results="hide"}
set.seed(sample.int(.Machine$integer.max, 1))
## Parametros
sigma2 <- sample(c(1, 2, 4, 9), 1)
n_obs  <- 25

## Resposta correta e alternativas incorretas
correct <- round(n_obs / sigma2, 4)
w1 <- round(sigma2 / n_obs, 4)
w2 <- round(n_obs * sigma2, 4)
w3 <- round(1 / (n_obs * sigma2), 4)
w4 <- round(n_obs / sqrt(sigma2), 4)

all_vals <- c(correct, w1, w2, w3, w4)
# Garante cinco alternativas numericamente distintas após a parametrização.
integer_choices <- all(abs(all_vals - round(all_vals)) < sqrt(.Machine$double.eps))
choice_step <- if (integer_choices) 1 else max(1e-4, max(abs(all_vals), na.rm = TRUE) * 0.01)
while (anyDuplicated(all_vals)) {
  duplicate_position <- which(duplicated(all_vals))[1]
  all_vals[duplicate_position] <- max(all_vals, na.rm = TRUE) + choice_step
}
choices <- sample(all_vals)
sol_pos <- which(choices == correct)
sol_str <- paste(as.integer(seq_along(choices) == sol_pos), collapse = "")
```

Question
========
Seja $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ com $\sigma^2 = `r sigma2`$ conhecido. A informacao de Fisher sobre $\mu$ para uma unica observacao e:
$$I(\mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} = `r round(1/sigma2, 4)`.$$

Para $n = `r n_obs`$ observacoes independentes, a informacao de Fisher total e:
$$I_n(\mu) = n \cdot I(\mu) = \frac{n}{\sigma^2}.$$

Qual e o valor de $I_{`r n_obs`}(\mu)$ para $\sigma^2 = `r sigma2`$?

Answerlist
----------
* `r choices[1]`
* `r choices[2]`
* `r choices[3]`
* `r choices[4]`
* `r choices[5]`

Solution
========
A informacao de Fisher para uma observacao $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ e:
$$I(\mu) = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\mu^2}\log f\right] = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}.$$

Para $n = `r n_obs`$ observacoes independentes:
$$I_{`r n_obs`}(\mu) = \frac{n}{\sigma^2} = \frac{`r n_obs`}{`r sigma2`} = `r correct`.$$

Note que a informacao e aditiva para amostras independentes.

Meta-information
================
exname: Cap4S2Q4_Fisher_Normal_mu
extype: schoice
exsolution: `r sol_str`
exshuffle: FALSE
